title: Das Räthsel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 22. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 22, one of the Grimms’ riddle-tales. A king’s son and his faithful servant lodge with a witch whose parting drink, spilled, poisons his horse; a raven eats the dead horse, and twelve murderers at an inn eat the raven — so a single poison, passed down the chain, slays all twelve. From this the prince makes an unanswerable riddle (“one slew none and yet slew twelve”) to win a haughty princess who beheads her failed suitors, and when she spies out the answer by night he catches her by her tell-tale grey cloak. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, ward, daß, wußte, Thier, todt, Thüren, Räthsel and übermüthig.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Königssohn, der bekam Lust in der Welt umher zu ziehen und nahm niemand mit als einen treuen Diener.
sentences.l: There was once a king’s son, who got a desire to travel about in the world and took no one along but one faithful servant.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a king’s son who took a fancy to roam about the world, and he took no one with him but one faithful servant.
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ “bekam Lust” — Lust bekommen = to get a desire, take a fancy to. “umher ziehen” (umherziehen, to roam about) is separable; the prefix umher sits before the zu-infinitive here.

sentences.t: Eines Tags gerieth er in einen großen Wald, und als der Abend kam, konnte er keine Herberge finden und wußte nicht wo er die Nacht zubringen sollte.
sentences.l: One day he got into a great forest, and when the evening came, he could find no lodging and did not know where he should pass the night.
sentences.i: One day he strayed into a great forest, and when evening came, he could find no shelter and did not know where he was to spend the night.
sentences.n: “gerieth” is the 1857 spelling of geriet (geraten, ‘to fall/stray into’); geraten in is to end up in a place or state by chance. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste. “zubringen” (to pass, spend time) is separable, held together here inside the wo-clause.

sentences.t: Da sah er ein Mädchen, das nach einem kleinen Häuschen zu gieng, und als er näher kam, sah er daß das Mädchen jung und schön war.
sentences.l: Then he saw a girl, that was going toward a little house, and when he came nearer, he saw that the girl was young and beautiful.
sentences.i: Then he saw a girl walking toward a little house, and when he came nearer, he saw that the girl was young and beautiful.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); the old -ie- recurs throughout the edition. “nach ... zu gehen” = to go toward. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Er redete es an und sprach „liebes Kind, kann ich und mein Diener in dem Häuschen für die Nacht ein Unterkommen finden?“
sentences.l: He addressed it and spoke, “dear child, can I and my servant find a lodging in the little house for the night?”
sentences.i: He spoke to her and said, “Dear child, can my servant and I find shelter in the little house for the night?”
sentences.n: “redete ... an” is the separable verb anreden (‘to address, speak to’). “es” refers to das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read as ‘her.’

sentences.t: „Ach ja,“ sagte das Mädchen mit trauriger Stimme, „das könnt ihr wohl, aber ich rathe euch nicht dazu; geht nicht hinein.“
sentences.l: “Oh yes,” said the girl with a sad voice, “that you can well do, but I do not advise you to it; do not go in.”
sentences.i: “Oh yes,” said the girl in a sad voice, “that you may well do, but I do not advise it; do not go inside.”
sentences.n: “rathe” is the 1857 spelling of rate (raten, ‘to advise’); raten takes the dative person (euch) — ‘I advise you.’ “wohl” here softens to ‘well, indeed.’

sentences.t: „Warum soll ich nicht?“ fragte der Königssohn.
sentences.l: “Why should I not?” asked the king’s son.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen seufzte und sprach „meine Stiefmutter treibt böse Künste, sie meints nicht gut mit den Fremden.“
sentences.l: The girl sighed and spoke, “my stepmother practices evil arts, she does not mean it well with the strangers.”
sentences.i: The girl sighed and said, “My stepmother practices black arts; she means no good to strangers.”
sentences.n: “böse Künste treiben” = to practice evil arts, work witchcraft (treiben here = to ply, carry on). “meints” = meint es, contracted; “es gut meinen mit” = to mean well by someone.

sentences.t: Da merkte er wohl daß er zu dem Haus einer Hexe gekommen war, doch weil es finster ward, und er nicht weiter konnte, sich auch nicht fürchtete, so trat er ein.
sentences.l: Then he well noticed that he had come to the house of a witch, yet because it grew dark, and he could go no further, was also not afraid, he stepped in.
sentences.i: Then he saw well enough that he had come to a witch’s house; but because it was growing dark, and he could go no further, and was not afraid either, he went in.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde. “finster” = dark, gloomy. “trat ... ein” (eintreten, to step in, enter) is separable.

sentences.t: Die Alte saß auf einem Lehnstuhl beim Feuer, und sah mit ihren rothen Augen die Fremden an.
sentences.l: The old woman sat on an armchair by the fire, and looked at the strangers with her red eyes.
sentences.i: The old woman sat in an armchair by the fire and looked at the strangers with her red eyes.
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). “rothen” is the 1857 spelling of roten (rot, ‘red’), with the old -th-. “sah ... an” (ansehen, to look at) is separable.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend,“ schnarrte sie, und that ganz freundlich, „laßt euch nieder, und ruht euch aus.“
sentences.l: “Good evening,” she rasped, and acted quite friendly, “sit yourselves down, and rest yourselves.”
sentences.i: “Good evening,” she rasped, putting on a very friendly manner, “sit down and rest yourselves.”
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = modern tun, ‘to do’); “ganz freundlich thun” = to act very friendly. “laßt ... nieder” (sich niederlassen, to sit down) and “ruht ... aus” (sich ausruhen, to rest) are separable reflexive imperatives. “laßt” keeps the old -ß.

sentences.t: Sie blies die Kohlen an, bei welchen sie in einem kleinen Topf etwas kochte.
sentences.l: She blew on the coals, by which she was cooking something in a little pot.
sentences.i: She blew up the coals, over which she was cooking something in a little pot.
sentences.n: “blies ... an” (anblasen, to blow on, fan) is separable; blies is the preterite of blasen. “bei welchen” = ‘by/at which’ (relative).

sentences.t: Die Tochter warnte die beiden vorsichtig zu sein, nichts zu essen und nichts zu trinken, denn die Alte braue böse Getränke.
sentences.l: The daughter warned the two to be careful, to eat nothing and to drink nothing, for the old woman brewed evil drinks.
sentences.i: The daughter warned the two of them to be careful, to eat nothing and drink nothing, for the old woman brewed evil potions.
sentences.n: “braue” is subjunctive I of brauen (‘to brew’) — the subjunctive of reported speech, marking the daughter’s warning as reported rather than the narrator’s own statement; this reported-speech subjunctive is everywhere in Grimm. “Getränke” = drinks, potions (plural of Getränk).

sentences.t: Sie schliefen ruhig bis zum frühen Morgen.
sentences.l: They slept peacefully until the early morning.

sentences.t: Als sie sich zur Abreise fertig machten und der Königssohn schon zu Pferde saß, sprach die Alte „warte, einen Augenblick, ich will euch erst einen Abschiedstrank reichen.“
sentences.l: When they made themselves ready for departure and the king’s son already sat on horseback, the old woman spoke, “wait, one moment, I will first hand you a farewell drink.”
sentences.i: When they were making ready to leave and the king’s son was already in the saddle, the old woman said, “Wait a moment — I will first hand you a parting cup.”
sentences.n: “zur Abreise fertig machen” = to make ready for departure. “zu Pferde sitzen” = to sit on horseback, be mounted (Pferde is the old dative). “Abschiedstrank” = a farewell drink, parting cup.

sentences.t: Während sie ihn holte, ritt der Königssohn fort, und der Diener, der seinen Sattel fest schnallen mußte, war allein noch zugegen, als die böse Hexe mit dem Trank kam.
sentences.l: While she fetched it, the king’s son rode off, and the servant, who had to buckle his saddle tight, was alone still present, when the evil witch came with the drink.
sentences.i: While she went to fetch it, the king’s son rode off, and the servant, who had to buckle his saddle tight, was the only one still there when the wicked witch came with the drink.
sentences.n: “ritt ... fort” (fortreiten, to ride off) is separable. “zugegen” = present, at hand (an archaic predicate word). “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste.

sentences.t: „Das bring deinem Herrn“ sagte sie, aber in dem Augenblick sprang das Glas und das Gift spritzte auf das Pferd, und war so heftig daß das Thier gleich todt hinstürzte.
sentences.l: “Bring this to your master,” she said, but in that moment the glass burst and the poison spurted onto the horse, and was so violent that the beast at once fell down dead.
sentences.i: “Take this to your master,” she said; but at that moment the glass burst and the poison splashed onto the horse, and it was so strong that the beast dropped down dead on the spot.
sentences.n: “bring” is the imperative of bringen; “deinem Herrn” is dative (‘to your master’). “sprang” here = burst, cracked (of the glass). “Thier” is the 1857 spelling of Tier (‘beast, animal’), with the old -th-; “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot (‘dead’). “hinstürzte” (hinstürzen, to crash down) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Diener lief seinem Herrn nach und erzählte ihm was geschehen war, wollte aber den Sattel nicht im Stich lassen und lief zurück um ihn zu holen.
sentences.l: The servant ran after his master and told him what had happened, but did not want to leave the saddle in the lurch and ran back to fetch it.
sentences.i: The servant ran after his master and told him what had happened, but he would not abandon the saddle and ran back to fetch it.
sentences.n: “lief ... nach” (nachlaufen, to run after, + dative) and “lief zurück” (zurücklaufen) are separable. “im Stich lassen” is a set idiom, ‘to leave in the lurch, abandon.’

sentences.t: Wie er aber zu dem todten Pferde kam, saß schon ein Rabe darauf und fraß davon.
sentences.l: But when he came to the dead horse, a raven already sat on it and ate of it.
sentences.i: But when he came to the dead horse, a raven was already sitting on it, eating away.
sentences.n: “todten” is the inflected 1857 form of todt (‘dead’); “Pferde” carries the old dative -e. “fraß” (fressen) is the verb for an animal’s eating — devouring, greedy — as opposed to essen for people. “davon” = of it, from it.

sentences.t: „Wer weiß ob wir heute noch etwas besseres finden,“ sagte der Diener, tödtete den Raben und nahm ihn mit.
sentences.l: “Who knows whether we shall find anything better today,” said the servant, killed the raven and took it along.
sentences.i: “Who knows whether we’ll find anything better today,” said the servant, and he killed the raven and took it along.
sentences.n: “tödtete” is the 1857 spelling of tötete (töten, ‘to kill’), with the old -dt-. “nahm ... mit” (mitnehmen, to take along) is separable. “etwas besseres” = something better.

sentences.t: Nun zogen sie in dem Walde den ganzen Tag weiter, konnten aber nicht heraus kommen.
sentences.l: Now they journeyed on in the forest the whole day, but could not come out.
sentences.i: Now they journeyed on through the forest all day long, but could not get out.
sentences.n: “zogen ... weiter” (weiterziehen, to journey on) is separable. “Walde” keeps the old dative -e. “heraus kommen” (herauskommen, to come out) is separable.

sentences.t: Bei Anbruch der Nacht fanden sie ein Wirthshaus und giengen hinein.
sentences.l: At the break of night they found an inn and went in.
sentences.i: At nightfall they found an inn and went inside.
sentences.n: “bei Anbruch der Nacht” = at nightfall (Anbruch = the breaking, onset). “Wirthshaus” is the 1857 spelling of Wirtshaus (‘inn’), with the old -th-. “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen.

sentences.t: Der Diener gab dem Wirth den Raben, den er zum Abendessen bereiten sollte.
sentences.l: The servant gave the innkeeper the raven, which he was to prepare for supper.
sentences.i: The servant gave the innkeeper the raven, for him to prepare for supper.
sentences.n: “Wirth” is the 1857 spelling of Wirt (‘innkeeper, host’). “dem Wirth” is dative (‘to the innkeeper’). “bereiten” = to prepare (a dish).

sentences.t: Sie waren aber in eine Mördergrube gerathen, und in der Dunkelheit kamen zwölf Mörder und wollten die Fremden umbringen und berauben.
sentences.l: But they had fallen into a murderers’ den, and in the darkness came twelve murderers and wanted to kill and rob the strangers.
sentences.i: But they had stumbled into a den of murderers, and in the darkness twelve murderers came and meant to kill the strangers and rob them.
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten (‘to fall into’); “in eine Mördergrube gerathen” = to fall into a murderers’ den. Note the twelve murderers here — hold on to that number; it is the answer to the riddle to come. “umbringen” (to kill) and “berauben” (to rob) round out their intent.

sentences.t: Eh sie sich aber ans Werk machten, setzten sie sich zu Tisch und der Wirth und die Hexe setzten sich zu ihnen, und sie aßen zusammen eine Schüssel mit Suppe, in die das Fleisch des Raben gehackt war.
sentences.l: But before they set themselves to the work, they sat down to table, and the innkeeper and the witch sat down with them, and they ate together a bowl of soup, into which the flesh of the raven had been chopped.
sentences.i: But before they set to work, they sat down at the table, and the innkeeper and the witch sat down with them, and together they ate a bowl of soup into which the raven’s flesh had been chopped.
sentences.n: “Eh” is a short form of ehe (‘before’). “sich ans Werk machen” = to set to work (Werk = work, task). “gehackt” is the past participle of hacken (‘to chop, mince’). Here the poison passes on: the raven ate the poisoned horse, and now its flesh is in the soup.

sentences.t: Kaum aber hatten sie ein paar Bissen hinunter geschluckt, so fielen sie alle todt nieder, denn dem Raben hatte sich das Gift von dem Pferdefleisch mitgetheilt.
sentences.l: But scarcely had they swallowed down a few mouthfuls, then they all fell down dead, for the poison from the horse-flesh had imparted itself to the raven.
sentences.i: But scarcely had they swallowed a few mouthfuls when they all fell down dead, for the poison had passed from the horse-flesh into the raven.
sentences.n: This is the hinge of the riddle: the poison went horse → raven → the men who ate the raven, so a single poisoning killed all twelve. “hinunter geschluckt” (hinunterschlucken, to swallow down) is separable. “sich mittheilen” (1857 spelling of mitteilen) here = to spread/pass to (+ dative: dem Raben, ‘to the raven’). “Pferdefleisch” = horse-flesh.

sentences.t: Es war nun niemand mehr im Hause übrig als die Tochter des Wirths, die es redlich meinte und an den gottlosen Dingen keinen Theil genommen hatte.
sentences.l: There was now no one left in the house but the innkeeper’s daughter, who meant it honestly and had taken no part in the godless doings.
sentences.i: Now there was no one left in the house but the innkeeper’s daughter, who was honest at heart and had taken no part in the godless doings.
sentences.n: “des Wirths” is the genitive of Wirth (‘of the innkeeper’). “es redlich meinen” = to be honest, mean well (redlich = honest, upright). “Theil” is the 1857 spelling of Teil; “an etwas Theil nehmen” = to take part in something.

sentences.t: Sie öffnete dem Fremden alle Thüren und zeigte ihm die angehäuften Schätze.
sentences.l: She opened all the doors to the stranger and showed him the heaped-up treasures.
sentences.i: She opened all the doors for the stranger and showed him the piled-up treasures.
sentences.n: “Thüren” is the 1857 spelling of Türen (‘doors’), with the old -th-. “dem Fremden” is dative (‘for/to the stranger’). “angehäuft” = heaped up, accumulated (past participle of anhäufen).

sentences.t: Der Königssohn aber sagte sie möchte alles behalten, er wollte nichts davon und ritt mit seinem Diener weiter.
sentences.l: But the king’s son said she might keep everything, he wanted none of it and rode on with his servant.
sentences.i: But the king’s son said she might keep it all; he wanted none of it, and rode on with his servant.
sentences.n: “möchte behalten” — subjunctive of reported speech: he said (that) she might keep it. “ritt ... weiter” (weiterreiten, to ride on) is separable.

sentences.t: Nachdem sie lange herum gezogen waren, kamen sie in eine Stadt, worin eine schöne aber übermüthige Königstochter war, die hatte bekannt machen lassen wer ihr ein Räthsel vorlegte das sie nicht errathen könnte, der sollte ihr Gemahl werden: erriethe sie es aber, so müßte er sich das Haupt abschlagen lassen.
sentences.l: After they had journeyed about a long time, they came into a city, wherein was a beautiful but haughty king’s daughter, who had had it made known: whoever laid before her a riddle that she could not guess, he should become her husband; but if she guessed it, then he must have his head struck off.
sentences.i: After they had wandered about for a long time, they came to a city where there lived a beautiful but arrogant princess, who had had it proclaimed: whoever set her a riddle she could not solve should become her husband; but if she guessed it, he must have his head struck off.
sentences.n: “übermüthig” is the 1857 spelling of übermütig (‘haughty, overweening’). “bekannt machen lassen” = to have (something) made known, proclaimed. “ein Räthsel vorlegen” = to put/pose a riddle (Räthsel is the 1857 spelling of Rätsel). “errathen” (1857 for erraten) = to guess (a riddle). “erriethe” is subjunctive II (‘if she should guess’). “sich das Haupt abschlagen lassen” = to have one’s head struck off — the causative lassen with a passive sense. “müßte” is the 1857 spelling of müsste.

sentences.t: Drei Tage hatte sie Zeit sich zu besinnen, sie war aber so klug daß sie immer die vorgelegten Räthsel vor der bestimmten Zeit errieth.
sentences.l: Three days she had time to reflect, but she was so clever that she always guessed the posed riddles before the appointed time.
sentences.i: She had three days’ time to think it over, but she was so clever that she always solved the riddles put to her before the set time was up.
sentences.n: “sich besinnen” = to reflect, deliberate. “die vorgelegten Räthsel” — vorgelegt is the past participle of vorlegen (‘put before, posed’), here used as an adjective. “errieth” is the 1857 spelling of erriet, the preterite of errathen/erraten (‘guessed’).

sentences.t: Schon waren neune auf diese Weise umgekommen, als der Königssohn anlangte und von ihrer großen Schönheit geblendet sein Leben daran setzen wollte.
sentences.l: Already nine had perished in this way, when the king’s son arrived and, dazzled by her great beauty, wanted to set his life upon it.
sentences.i: Nine men had already perished in this way when the king’s son arrived and, dazzled by her great beauty, was willing to stake his life on it.
sentences.n: “neune” is an older form of neun (‘nine’) with an added -e. “auf diese Weise” = in this way (Weise = way, manner). “umgekommen” (umkommen, to perish) is separable. “anlangte” = arrived (anlangen). “geblendet” = dazzled, blinded (from blenden). “sein Leben daran setzen” = to stake his life on it.

sentences.t: Da trat er vor sie hin und gab ihr sein Räthsel auf, „was ist das,“ sagte er, „einer schlug keinen und schlug doch zwölfe.“
sentences.l: Then he stepped before her and gave her his riddle, “what is this,” he said, “one slew none and yet slew twelve.”
sentences.i: Then he stepped up before her and set her his riddle: “What is this,” he said, “one slew none, and yet slew twelve.”
sentences.n: “trat ... hin” (hintreten, to step up) and “gab ... auf” (aufgeben, here ‘to set, pose’ a riddle) are separable. The riddle: “einer schlug keinen und schlug doch zwölfe” — schlug is the preterite of schlagen (‘to strike, slay’). “zwölfe” is an older form of zwölf (‘twelve’) with -e. The solution unfolds below: the raven (which killed no one directly) carried the poison that killed all twelve murderers.

sentences.t: Sie wußte nicht was das war, sie sann und sann, aber sie brachte es nicht heraus: sie schlug ihre Räthselbücher auf, aber es stand nicht darin: kurz ihre Weisheit war zu Ende.
sentences.l: She did not know what that was, she pondered and pondered, but she did not bring it out: she opened her riddle-books, but it did not stand therein: in short, her wisdom was at an end.
sentences.i: She did not know what it was; she pondered and pondered, but she could not work it out; she opened her riddle-books, but it was not in them; in short, her wisdom was at an end.
sentences.n: “sann” is the preterite of sinnen (‘to ponder, muse’). “brachte ... heraus” (herausbringen, here ‘to work out’) is separable. “schlug ... auf” (aufschlagen, to open a book) is separable. “Räthselbücher” = riddle-books. “Weisheit” = wisdom. “zu Ende” = at an end.

sentences.t: Da sie sich nicht zu helfen wußte, befahl sie ihrer Magd in das Schlafgemach des Herrn zu schleichen, da sollte sie seine Träume behorchen, und dachte er rede vielleicht im Schlaf und verrathe das Räthsel.
sentences.l: Since she did not know how to help herself, she ordered her maid to creep into the master’s bedchamber, there she should eavesdrop on his dreams, and thought he might perhaps talk in his sleep and betray the riddle.
sentences.i: Since she was at her wits’ end, she ordered her maid to creep into the gentleman’s bedchamber and there to listen in on his dreams, thinking that perhaps he talked in his sleep and would give the riddle away.
sentences.n: “Da” here = ‘since, because.’ “sich nicht zu helfen wissen” = to be at a loss, not know what to do. “schleichen” = to creep, sneak; “Schlafgemach” = bedchamber. “behorchen” = to eavesdrop on, listen in on. “rede” and “verrathe” are subjunctive I of reported thought (reden, ‘to talk’; verrathen, 1857 for verraten, ‘to betray, give away’) — the reported-speech subjunctive again. “Träume” = dreams.

sentences.t: Aber der kluge Diener hatte sich statt des Herrn ins Bett gelegt und als die Magd heran kam, riß er ihr den Mantel ab, in den sie sich verhüllt hatte, und jagte sie mit Ruthen hinaus.
sentences.l: But the clever servant had laid himself in the bed instead of the master, and when the maid came near, he tore off her the cloak in which she had wrapped herself, and drove her out with rods.
sentences.i: But the clever servant had lain down in the bed in place of his master; and when the maid came near, he tore off the cloak in which she had wrapped herself and drove her out with rods.
sentences.n: “statt des Herrn” = instead of the master (genitive). “kam ... heran” (herankommen, to approach) is separable. “riß ... ab” (abreißen, to tear off) is separable; “ihr” is a dative of possession — ‘tore the cloak off her.’ “Mantel” = cloak; “sich verhüllen” = to wrap/veil oneself. “jagte ... hinaus” (hinausjagen, to drive out) is separable; “Ruthen” is the 1857 spelling of Ruten (‘rods, switches’).

sentences.t: In der zweiten Nacht schickte die Königstochter ihre Kammerjungfer, die sollte sehen ob es ihr mit Horchen besser glückte, aber der Diener nahm auch ihr den Mantel weg, und jagte sie mit Ruthen hinaus.
sentences.l: On the second night the king’s daughter sent her chamber-maid, who was to see whether it succeeded better for her with listening, but the servant took away from her too the cloak, and drove her out with rods.
sentences.i: On the second night the princess sent her chambermaid, who was to see whether she had better luck at eavesdropping; but the servant took her cloak away as well, and drove her out with rods.
sentences.n: “die sollte sehen” — the demonstrative die resumes the chambermaid (‘she was to see’). “Horchen” = listening, eavesdropping (the verb horchen used as a noun). “es glückte ihr” = it succeeded for her, she had luck (impersonal, + dative). “nahm ... weg” (wegnehmen, to take away) is separable; “ihr” is again dative.

sentences.t: Nun glaubte der Herr für die dritte Nacht sicher zu sein und legte sich in sein Bett, da kam die Königstochter selbst, hatte einen nebelgrauen Mantel umgethan und setzte sich neben ihn.
sentences.l: Now the master believed himself safe for the third night and lay down in his bed; then came the king’s daughter herself, had put on a mist-grey cloak and sat down beside him.
sentences.i: Now the gentleman thought himself safe for the third night and lay down in his bed; then the princess came herself, having put on a mist-grey cloak, and sat down beside him.
sentences.n: “nebelgrau” = mist-grey (Nebel ‘mist’ + grau ‘grey’) — this grey cloak becomes the decisive proof. “umgethan” is the 1857 spelling of umgetan, the past participle of umthun (‘to put on, wrap around’). “legte sich” / “setzte sich” are reflexive.

sentences.t: Und als sie dachte er schliefe und träumte, so redete sie ihn an und hoffte er werde im Traume antworten, wie viele thun: aber er war wach und verstand und hörte alles sehr wohl.
sentences.l: And when she thought he was sleeping and dreaming, she addressed him and hoped he would answer in his dream, as many do: but he was awake and understood and heard everything very well.
sentences.i: And when she thought he was asleep and dreaming, she spoke to him and hoped he would answer in his dream, as many people do; but he was awake, and understood and heard everything perfectly well.
sentences.n: “schliefe ... träumte” are subjunctive (her supposition); “werde ... antworten” is subjunctive I of her hope — all reported-speech subjunctive. “redete ... an” (anreden) is separable. “träumte” from träumen (‘to dream’); “im Traume” has the old dative -e. “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun.

sentences.t: Da fragte sie „einer schlug keinen, was ist das?“
sentences.l: Then she asked, “one slew none, what is that?”

sentences.t: Er antwortete „ein Rabe der von einem todten und vergifteten Pferde fraß und davon starb.“
sentences.l: He answered, “a raven that ate of a dead and poisoned horse and died from it.”
sentences.i: He answered, “A raven that ate of a dead, poisoned horse and died of it.”
sentences.n: This is the first half of the riddle’s solution — the raven ‘slew none.’ “antworten” = to answer. “todten ... vergifteten” are inflected past-participle adjectives, ‘dead’ and ‘poisoned’ (vergiften, to poison); “Pferde” keeps the old dative -e. “davon starb” = died from it.

sentences.t: Weiter fragte sie „und schlug doch zwölfe, was ist das?“
sentences.l: Further she asked, “and yet slew twelve, what is that?”
sentences.i: She asked on, “and yet slew twelve — what is that?”

sentences.t: „Das sind zwölf Mörder, die den Raben verzehrten und daran starben.“
sentences.l: “Those are twelve murderers, who consumed the raven and died from it.”
sentences.i: “Those are twelve murderers who ate the raven and died of it.”
sentences.n: The second half of the solution: the poison, carried by the raven, killed all twelve. “Mörder” = murderers. “verzehrten” = consumed, ate up (verzehren). “daran starben” = died of it.

sentences.t: Als sie das Räthsel wußte, wollte sie sich fortschleichen, aber er hielt ihren Mantel fest, daß sie ihn zurücklassen mußte.
sentences.l: When she knew the riddle, she wanted to steal away, but he held her cloak fast, so that she had to leave it behind.
sentences.i: When she had the riddle, she wanted to slip away, but he held her cloak fast, so that she had to leave it behind.
sentences.n: “sich fortschleichen” = to steal/slink away (separable, reflexive). “hielt ... fest” (festhalten, to hold fast) is separable. “zurücklassen” = to leave behind. The captured grey cloak will convict her.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen verkündigte die Königstochter sie habe das Räthsel errathen, und ließ die zwölf Richter kommen und löste es vor ihnen.
sentences.l: On the next morning the king’s daughter announced she had guessed the riddle, and had the twelve judges come and solved it before them.
sentences.i: The next morning the princess announced that she had guessed the riddle, and she summoned the twelve judges and solved it before them.
sentences.n: “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern is the old form of anderen). “verkündigte” = announced, proclaimed. “sie habe ... errathen” — subjunctive I of reported claim (‘that she had guessed it’). “ließ ... kommen” = had (them) come, summoned. “löste” = solved (lösen, here of solving the riddle).

sentences.t: Aber der Jüngling bat sich Gehör aus, und sagte „sie ist in der Nacht zu mir geschlichen und hat mich ausgefragt, denn sonst hätte sie es nicht errathen.“
sentences.l: But the youth begged himself a hearing, and said, “she crept to me in the night and questioned me out, for otherwise she would not have guessed it.”
sentences.i: But the young man asked leave to speak, and said, “She crept to me in the night and questioned me, for otherwise she could never have guessed it.”
sentences.n: “sich Gehör ausbitten” = to ask for a hearing, ask leave to speak (ausbitten is separable). “geschlichen” is the past participle of schleichen (‘crept’). “ausgefragt” (ausfragen, to question closely, interrogate) is separable. “hätte ... errathen” is subjunctive II — the counterfactual ‘she would not have guessed.’

sentences.t: Die Richter sprachen „bringt uns ein Wahrzeichen.“
sentences.l: The judges spoke, “bring us a token.”
sentences.i: The judges said, “Bring us a token of proof.”
sentences.n: “bringt” is the plural imperative (‘bring, all of you’). “Wahrzeichen” = a token, sign, proof.

sentences.t: Da wurden die drei Mäntel von dem Diener herbei gebracht, und als die Richter den nebelgrauen erblickten, den die Königstochter zu tragen pflegte, so sagten sie „laßt den Mantel sticken mit Gold und Silber, so wirds euer Hochzeitsmantel sein.“
sentences.l: Then the three cloaks were brought forth by the servant, and when the judges caught sight of the mist-grey one, which the king’s daughter was accustomed to wear, they said, “have the cloak embroidered with gold and silver, so it will be your wedding cloak.”
sentences.i: Then the three cloaks were brought forward by the servant, and when the judges caught sight of the mist-grey one, which the princess was wont to wear, they said, “Have the cloak embroidered with gold and silver, and it shall be your wedding cloak.”
sentences.n: “Mäntel” is the plural of Mantel (‘cloaks’). “herbei gebracht” (herbeibringen, to bring forth) is separable. “den nebelgrauen” = the mist-grey one (adjective as noun). “zu tragen pflegte” — pflegen + zu = to be accustomed to (‘was wont to wear’). “laßt ... sticken” = have (it) embroidered (sticken, to embroider). “wirds” = wird es (‘it will’); “Hochzeitsmantel” = wedding cloak.
